task_completion.description: Verify that a web browser is open.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A web browser window (like Firefox) is visible on the screen.

task_completion.description: Verify that the YDBGui web interface is loaded in the browser.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The YDBGui dashboard or interface is visible in the browser tab, indicating successful connection to the database interface.
The string '^PS(51.2)' is visible in the YDBGui global explorer, navigation tree, or query input field, showing the agent has accessed the Medication Routes file.
The text 'INTRAVENOUS' is visible in the data view or results area of the YDBGui interface, specifically within the context of the ^PS(51.2) global.

task_completion.description: Verify the abbreviation 'IV' is displayed for the INTRAVENOUS entry.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The text 'IV' is visible in the same record, row, or expanded data node as the 'INTRAVENOUS' entry.

integrity.description: Ensure the agent used the YDBGui web interface rather than terminal commands.
integrity.visual_evidence: The data should be displayed within the graphical YDBGui web interface in a browser, not in a terminal emulator or SSH session using raw MUMPS commands.

integrity.description: Ensure the agent did not just type the expected answers into a text editor.
integrity.visual_evidence: The screen should not show a text editor (like nano, vim, or gedit) containing the words 'INTRAVENOUS' and 'IV'. The text must appear within the actual database interface.

integrity.description: Ensure the results appear to come from genuine software interaction.
integrity.visual_evidence: The layout of the data should match a database explorer view (e.g., tree nodes, tables, or key-value pairs typical of YDBGui), rather than a web search result or manually constructed HTML page.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The target interface is YDBGui, accessed via a web browser (Firefox on port 8089). The VistA MEDICATION ROUTES dictionary corresponds to file 51.2 and is stored in the ^PS(51.2) global. The standard record name to look for is 'INTRAVENOUS', and its corresponding abbreviation in the database is 'IV'.
